sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A gerontology study examined 180 family caregivers of older adults with dementia. Caregivers were grouped by weekly respite-care use: none, 1–3 hours, or 4 or more hours. Mean caregiver-burden scores on a 0–40 scale were 29, 24, and 19, respectively. Mean depressive-symptom scores were 15, 12, and 9. Caregivers using more respite also reported more contact with support groups and greater confidence navigating community services.
questions.stem: Which group reported the lowest caregiver burden?

choices.B: Caregivers using 4 or more hours of respite care weekly
choices.C: Caregivers using 1–3 hours of respite care
choices.D: All groups had identical burden scores

explanations.B: Correct. The 4-or-more-hours group had a mean burden score of 19, lower than 24 for the 1–3-hour group and 29 for the no-respite group.
explanations.A: The passage does not support caregivers using no respite care; it points instead to caregivers using 4 or more hours of respite care weekly.
explanations.C: The passage does not support caregivers using 1–3 hours of respite care; it points instead to caregivers using 4 or more hours of respite care weekly.
explanations.D: The passage does not support all groups had identical burden scores; it points instead to caregivers using 4 or more hours of respite care weekly.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Caregiver-burden and depressive-symptom scores by weekly respite-care use.

questions.stem: Respite care most directly provides which kind of support?

explanations.C: Correct. Respite care provides practical help by temporarily relieving caregiving duties, which is instrumental support.
explanations.A: The passage does not support the mere-exposure effect; it points instead to instrumental support.
explanations.B: The passage does not support sensory adaptation; it points instead to instrumental support.
explanations.D: The passage does not support conservation; it points instead to instrumental support.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.A: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.B: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.C: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.A: Correct. Caregivers were grouped by existing respite use, and those with more respite also had more support-group contact, so resources may be confounded.
explanations.B: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.C: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.D: Expand respite access and connect caregivers to support resources
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.B: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.C: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.D: Correct. The lower burden and depressive symptoms among respite users suggest agencies should support caregiver relief and resource navigation.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.B: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.C: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.C: Greater respite use was associated with lower caregiver burden and depressive symptoms
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.C: Correct. This conclusion matches the group pattern while avoiding the claim that respite alone caused every difference.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.

questions.passage: A community center offered an 8-week cognitive training program for adults aged 70 and older. Participants chose memory-strategy training, processing-speed games, or a social book-discussion group. Memory-strategy participants improved most on delayed word recall, processing-speed participants improved most on a symbol-search task, and book-discussion participants reported the largest increase in social connectedness. The program director emphasized that different activities may benefit different domains rather than producing one global improvement.
questions.stem: Which group improved most on delayed word recall?

choices.D: All groups improved most on the same task

explanations.B: Correct. The passage states that memory-strategy participants improved most on delayed word recall, matching the skill their activity targeted.
explanations.A: The passage does not support processing-speed game participants; it points instead to memory-strategy training participants.
explanations.C: The passage does not support book-discussion participants; it points instead to memory-strategy training participants.
explanations.D: The passage does not support all groups improved most on the same task; it points instead to memory-strategy training participants.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Improvement scores across memory, speed, and connectedness outcomes for three activity groups.

questions.stem: The pattern of different gains across groups most directly suggests what?

choices.A: Every activity improves every cognitive domain equally
choices.B: Older adults cannot benefit from cognitive activity
choices.C: Social connection is irrelevant to well-being

explanations.D: Correct. Each activity produced its strongest effect in the domain it most directly practiced or supported, consistent with domain-specific benefits.
explanations.A: The passage does not support every activity improves every cognitive domain equally; it points instead to training effects may be domain-specific.
explanations.B: The passage does not support older adults cannot benefit from cognitive activity; it points instead to training effects may be domain-specific.
explanations.C: The passage does not support social connection is irrelevant to well-being; it points instead to training effects may be domain-specific.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.A: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.B: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.C: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.A: Correct. Participants chose their programs, so preexisting interests or abilities could contribute to outcome differences across groups.
explanations.B: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.C: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.B: Offer activities aligned with different goals such as memory, speed, and social connection
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.C: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.B: Correct. A menu of options matches the passage’s message that different activities benefit different domains.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.C: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.C: Cognitive and social activities may produce different benefits in older adults rather than one global improvement
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.C: Correct. This conclusion follows from the domain-specific pattern and avoids overstating causality.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.
